data.title: El Al Israel Airlines: Speculative Buy Opportunity With Risk Of Competition
data.description:
El Al Israel Airlines Ltd. (OTC:ELALF) has surged 45.5% since my last report for investors in January. The stock has not yet reached my price target of $4.32, but I do believe it is time for an update on the stock as dynamics that supported significantly higher stock prices are changing. It should be noted that the OTC listing has gained 45% since my last coverage bringing the stock price to $3.71, but the listing of El Al at the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange “only” gained 20%. This is driven by lower volumes on the OTC ticker which may result in price making that at times is not reflective of the price changes for the underlying instrument.

In this report, I will be discussing the most recent results, update on the risk and opportunities and update my price target for El Al stock.

El Al Shows Huge Earnings Growth

Revenues increased strongly in 2024 to $3.4 billion. This increase was driven by an 11.8% increase in capacity and higher unit revenues as well as an increase in cargo activities. Total operating expenses grew by 18.9%, which was higher than the capacity expansion. System unit costs excluding fuel increased by 9.6%. The operating profit increased by 189.1% to $772.7 million with net earnings growing from $116.7 million to $544.9 million.

The Risks For El Al Airlines

The results of El Al Airlines have without doubt been impressive and the stock price performance year-to-date also shows as much. If we look at the main carriers in the US, we see that those have all declined. That is driven by the trade turmoil.

El Al’s stock performance is diverging from that. One simple reason would be that it is not a US carrier and therefore it is less affected by the trade turmoil. The other reason is simply because it had a unique positioning in connecting Israel to the world since the war in Gaza started. When the war started, airlines pulled out of Israel leaving El Al to service demand. El Al had a tailwind from that including increased demand for flying reservists into Israel as well as cargo.

That may very well become the risk for El Al going forward. The war is not over nor do we see strong signs that tensions in the region will ease in the near-term. However, airlines are restarting flights to Israel. United (UAL) restarted flights in March and Delta restarted on the first of April. American Airlines has not yet announced a resumption of service, but a flurry of European operators have. So, the marketplace is expected to look significantly different in 2025 and that may not be in the benefit of El Al.

El Al Stock Price Target Revised Downward

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For El Al, I am basing my price target on the stock price at the Tel Aviv Stock Exchange converted to USD. That price target is $3.83 which implies 29% upside. However, against the OTC listing the upside is merely 3.2%. So, the OTC listing offers a less compelling investment at this point, but the underlying instrument remains attractive.

While I do see downward risk to earnings in the years ahead as the marketplace normalizes for El Al leading to lower unit revenues, the company seems to be much better capitalized to service its debt. I do note that the company needs to show strong cost control as the company is unlikely to offset cost growth by strong unit revenue growth in the new set up were international carriers will service part of the air travel demand.

My stock price target has decreased from $4.32 to $3.83 to align with earnings pressure exerted by other airlines re-entering the market.

Conclusion: El Al Israel Airlines Stock Has Upside But Risk Is Increasing

I do believe that El Al Airlines stock continues to have upside, but with international airlines restarting services to Israel there will be pressure on unit revenues and earnings providing a significant downside risk to the estimates. So, the buy rating is speculative and I do have to point out that upside has been lowered for 2025 and shifted to the right thereafter.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: MSCI: A Top Pick Amid Tariff Uncertainty
data.description:
Within the financial sector, the stock of MSCI (NYSE:MSCI) has been one of the outperformers amid the market turmoil. Their asset-light business, high client switching costs, and no direct impact from tariffs are preferred attributes for investors during this market environment.

MSCI's core business is licensing market indices to asset managers. For the most part, they charge a variable fee to the funds licensing their indices based on their AUM. At the same time, they offer market data solutions to financial institutions. Half of their client base are asset managers, but they also serve banks, hedge funds, and consultants.

Since the market peak of February 19, the S&P 500 has dropped approximately -14.25%, followed by the financial sector -10.83%, and MSCI, which has outperformed, dropping -8.13%. Although MSCI is not immune to a GDP slowdown, the degree to which it is affected is likely much lower than, for example, a bank, which might incur excess provisions for loan losses. At the same time, MSCI has more than 7,100 institutional clients across more than 100 countries, and their global indices are popular among foreign asset managers.

MSCI Ability To Generate Free Cash Flow

MSCI is an asset-light business that does not require much investment in maintenance capex and has a high net income margin, with the most recent one of 38.83%. With a strong revenue rise over the years, the firm has consolidated a CAGR of 13%, which has also translated to the growth in free cash flow with a CAGR of 16%. As a consequence, the company is able to deliver these returns in a mix of dividends and share repurchases. Over the past six years, the share count has been reduced by -12.2%, and the quarterly dividend has been increased by 210%.

MSCI: Shares Remain Undervalued

In my previous analysis of MSCI, I concluded that its shares were undervalued. Although the shares of MSCI haven't dropped considerably compared to the rest of the market, the undervaluation still holds and has slightly expanded.Currently, MSCI trades at 30.9x price-to-free cash flow, which is a -27% difference from the five-year historical average of 42.2x.

At the same time, based on a discounted cash flow model, the shares have an intrinsic value of $638, which implies an upside potential of 14.6% based on Tuesday's stock closing price. The model considers the revenue estimates for the next three years and thereafter assumes a 7.5% growth in the remaining years, holding the ratio averages steady. It also supposes a 5% perpetual growth rate of the free cash flow and a discount rate of 8.5% based on CAPM.

Risks From AUMs Dropping and Potential ESG Shift in Europe

With global asset levels dropping considerably, MSCI will probably experience an effect on its top line as asset managers would be required to pay less fees for licensing MSCI's indices. In 2024, asset-based fees were $657.501 million and grew 17.9% from the prior year due to higher AUMs. At the same time, financial institutions might be incentivized to reduce expenses, and one way to do this is by reducing the budget for subscriptions. Last year, MSCI achieved $2.114 billion in recurring subscriptions, which grew 13% YoY. Nonetheless, subscription cancellations saw a rise of $131.510 million during the year and were up 41.1% YoY.

For an asset manager, subscriptions are substantially easier to discontinue than index licensing, which might remain till the termination of a fund due to the complexity of changing a benchmark. Nonetheless, it is possible that seeking capital might become more complex in this environment, and funds to be launched by asset managers might be delayed or canceled.

Another risk is their Climate & ESG segment, which constituted 11.4% of the operating revenues in 2024. For this, MSCI has developed a large range of tools for asset managers to obtain ESG data and link it to their portfolios. Nonetheless, the future of ESG remains uncertain, with strong outflows in the United States.

The largest demand for ESG funds comes from Europe, which constitutes approximately 56% of the equity AUMs. Still, with the war in Ukraine and the lack of support from the Trump administration for European defense, Europeans have started to rethink their asset allocations and become more flexible. Germany, for example, has approved a spending package in military defense and infrastructure, exceeding 1% of their GDP. Weapons, for example, are negatively screened from most ESG funds, but asset managers, such as UBS, Allianz, and Danske Bank, have started to adjust their policies.

Takeaway

My previous ratings of MSCI have all been a strong buy. This is a business that is powerful in the Porter's Five Forces framework and has an enviable operating leverage with an operating margin of 53.72% that has considerably expanded through the years, when it was at 37.57% in 2015. Their ability to generate and grow free cash flow has been substantial through the years, and demonstrates the quality of the business. In addition, they have a shareholder-friendly management that has spent $3.764 billion in share repurchases over the past five years, to the point that the equity of the business became negative.

Although the drawdown isn't as appealing compared to the rest of the market, the valuation is now cheaper and continues to be undervalued. At the same time, if market drops continue, it is likely that MSCI won't be as affected as they don't have a direct impact from tariffs, and most of their revenue is recurrent. For the reasons mentioned above, I will continue to rate the shares of MSCI as a "strong buy."

data.title: Morgan Stanley Direct Lending: 10% Yield And 114% Dividend Coverage Make It A Buy
data.description:
High yield is sometimes associated with higher risk. While that may be true for some standard C Corporations, it’s not necessarily the case across all asset sectors. In particular, BDCs are by design structured to distribute 90% of their taxable income to shareholders such that the corporate entity doesn’t need to pay income taxes.

While individual shareholders need to pay taxes on their dividends received, that can be deferred if a BDC is held in a tax advantaged retirement account like an IRA or Roth IRA. Moreover, high income can be a great buffer against market volatility.

Such I find the case to be with Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (NYSE:MSDL), which I last covered in January, highlighting its well-constructed and conservatively managed portfolio, which helps in these volatile times. Despite the stock price having declined in price, MSDL’s total return of -6% (including dividends) since my last piece is nearly 50% better than the 11.2% decline in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe. At the current price of $19.33, MSDL well in the bottom half of its 52-week range and carries a dividend yield well above 10%.

In this article, I revisit MSDL including its business fundamentals and valuation and discuss what makes it a great ‘Buy’ on the dip for value and income, so let’s dive in!

Why MSDL?

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending is a BDC that’s externally managed by Morgan Stanley (MS). This affiliation enables MSDL to leverage the investment bank’s extensive sponsor relationships and deal origination capabilities.

MSDL adopts a conservative lending approach toward U.S. middle market companies. This is reflected by 97% of its portfolio being in the form of first lien senior secured debt with a weighted average loan-to-value ratio of 40%. This helps to ensure a significant amount of equity to buffer against potential losses. Plus, MSDL’s investments have private equity sponsors or comparable market participants, which helps to ensure an additional layer of due diligence (since PE firms have more to lose) and management guidance for portfolio companies.

Moreover, MS has good alignment of interest with MSDL due to its 11% ownership stake. It also charges a below industry standard base management fee of 1.0%, comparing favorably to the 1.5% fee of industry bellwether, Ares Capital (ARCC). MSDL’s portfolio is also well diversified with 95% non-cyclical investments.

The average position size makes up just 50 basis points of the portfolio total with no borrower comprising over 2%. As shown below, the top sectors represent a blend of growth and defensive including software, insurance, commercial services & supplies, and IT services, which make up nearly half the portfolio.

It’s no secret that a decline in interest rates has impacted MSDL’s portfolio income, due to 99.6% of MSDL’s credit portfolio being tied to floating rate loans. This is reflected by NII per share declining by $0.10 on a YoY basis to $0.57 in Q4 2024. This is driven by the weighted average yield on cost declining by 160 bps from 12% at the end of 2023 to 10.4% at the end of last year. Importantly for income investors, MSDL’s NII continues to cover the regular $0.50 dividend rate with a 114% coverage ratio.

It’s worth noting that the silver lining to lower rates is that they have attracted more borrowers coming out of the woodwork. While the federal base interest rate declined by 100 basis points last year, MSDL’s portfolio grew by a robust 19% YoY to $3.8 billion fair value. This was driven by $574 million worth of net funded investment activity last year. As shown below, over 99% of new fundings over the past 5 quarters were in first-lien secured debt, bringing the total percentage of portfolio first lien from 94% to 97%.

At the same time, MSDL maintains solid portfolio credit quality with 98.3% of investments having an internal credit rating of 1 or 2 (on a scale from 1 to 4, with 1 being the highest quality). Since mid-2024, MSDL’s percentage of investments with a 3 or 4 rating declined by 20 basis points to 1.7%.

Plus, investments on non-accrual remain low at just 0.2% of portfolio cost. Measuring non-accrual as a percentage of portfolio cost is generally better than portfolio fair value, since the former is a better measure of long-term investment performance (i.e. results are not diluted by recent portfolio growth). Moreover, NAV per share remained steady, declining by just $0.02 sequentially to $20.81. As shown below, MSDL’s NAV/share is up significantly since its inception in 2023.

Risks to MSDL include the potential for lower interest rates should the economy hit a snag due to pressures from tariffs. Economic pressures could also lower borrower demand. In addition, MSDL’s PIK income has grown from $2 million in 2023 to $3 million last year, as shown below. PIK income refers to payment-in-kind, in which interest payments are not made by the borrower in cash but rather rolled into the balance of the loan. As one can imagine, PIK income is a riskier type of loan and I would monitor to see if PIK income rises relative to the overall portfolio in the coming quarters.

MSDL maintains a sound balance sheet with Baa3/BBB- credit ratings from Moody’s and Fitch. This is supported by a debt to equity ratio of 1.08x, which is higher by 9 basis points sequentially but far lower than the 2.0x statutory limit for BDCs.

Lastly, I continue to find MSDL to be appealing at the current price of $19.33 with a price-to-NAV of 0.93x, equating to a 7% discount from NAV. The discount seems to be too high given the conservatively managed portfolio comprised of first-lien loans, strong balance sheet and steady portfolio performance with a low non-accrual rate. Plus, with a dividend that has a 114% coverage ratio based on NII, MSDL offers a mix of value and high income.

As shown below, MSDL is the cheapest among other large BDC peers Ares Capital, Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), which carry P/NAV ratios in the 1.05 to 1.19x range.

Investor Takeaway

Morgan Stanley Direct Lending stands out as a compelling high-yield investment, especially in today’s volatile market. Despite a recent dip in share price, MSDL’s total return has outperformed the broader market. This is thanks to its high yield that’s backed by a conservatively managed, first-lien-focused portfolio and strong underwriting discipline.

Backed by Morgan Stanley’s deep sponsor network and a well-covered 10.3% dividend yield, MSDL offers both high income and buffers against portfolio losses. At its 7% discount to NAV and with minimal credit losses, MSDL presents an attractive opportunity for value and income investors at the current price.

data.title: Reel Relief: Escaping Economic Angst At The Movies
data.description:
With all that’s happening with tariff uncertainty, let’s lighten the mood a bit. How does a long holiday weekend with an afternoon trip to the movies sound? The Wall Street Horizon team is totally in. Springtime is here, the sun will rise again, and Hollywood is cranking out some gems. Join us.

Improving Numbers

According to Box Office Mojo by IMDbPro, “A Minecraft Movie” has revived 2025’s motion picture storyline. Through April 13, the Warner Bros. (WBD) film grossed over $280 million. It has led ticket sales for 10 days running through last Sunday, helping to bring this year’s US box office revenue to $1.85 billion, about on par with the same period in 2024. Through March, 2025 had lagged year-ago sales by more than 11%, per Box Office Mojo.1

Strong Sales Ahead? Or Will the Industry Lay an Egg?

All eyes are now on Easter weekend, which includes Good Friday - a bank holiday and an opportunity for families to get out to the theaters. There's a modest sense of optimism that even if a macro slowdown transpires, consumers may keep treating themselves to small and local excursions, and movies can be a reliable low-budget get-out-of-the-house entertainment option.

Larger fun and leisure expenses, such as sporting events and concerts, or protracted discretionary categories, like overseas vacations, could be the first to get slashed from household budgets.

This Year Tracks 2024’s YTD Ticket Sales

Source: IMDbPro Box Office Mojo

To cap off 2024, Disney (DIS) and Marvel's (MRVL) "Deadpool & Wolverine," Pixar's "Inside Out 2," Disney Animation's "Moana 2" and Universal's (UVV) "Wicked" collectively delivered a box office boost. Full-year ticket sales were lower year-on-year, however, reaching just $8.74 billion domestically in 2024.2

Disney snatched the crowd for the most movie-goer revenue, claiming 25% of US ticket sales.3 According to CNBC, 2025’s box office will likely fall shy of the $10 billion mark and remain below the $11 billion pre-COVID benchmark. Internationally, though, it’s a different tale.4

The Big China Story

“Ne Zha 2” has been an absolute blockbuster. Investors with their heads in the trading sand following the market’s day-to-day might not know about this one. Released on Valentine’s Day in the US, it has grossed a paltry $20.9 million. Totals from Europe, Middle East, and Africa (EMEA) and Asia Pacific (APAC) are unimpressive, too.5 But in China, it’s a gaudy gross at $1.86 billion, making global sales likely to top $2 billion before long.6

And that’s an important point - the movie industry isn’t centered within North American borders any longer. Indeed, US exceptionalism (in this instance) is a relic of the past. Moreover, the at-home entertainment dimension is a force to be reckoned with. Major players like Disney, Universal, and Paramount (PARA) continue to battle convenient streaming options dominated by Netflix (NFLX) and Amazon (AMZN).

Netflix Takes a Backseat

Our data show that Netflix may be chilling on its movie production pace. The $393 billion market cap Communication Services sector company stole the show in 2024. Over the next four months or so - through the July 4 holiday and much of the summer - it's slated to release just eight major motion pictures.

On the other hand, Lionsgate Entertainment (LGF.A, LGF.B) is expected to produce 11 big movies through early August. Comcast (CMCSA), Warner Bros, Disney, and Sony Group (SONY) are also prolific over the rest of the second quarter through early Q3.

Upcoming Movie Release Count

Source: Wall Street Horizon

“A Minecraft Movie” tops the 2025 box office, but there have been other success stories. Disney’s “Captain America: Brave New World” will almost certainly eclipse the $200 million mark Easter weekend, while “Mufasa: The Lion King” has notched $126 million in sales this year after tallying just under $100 million last December.7

New Releases

Entertainment analysts now look forward to fresh titles. “Captain America: Brave New World” debuted digitally earlier this week, along with Amazon’s “A Working Man” and Universal’s “A Woman in the Yard.” Netflix is slated to release iHostage on Good Friday; Warner Bros is banking on “Sinners” to score big numbers - it hits the silver screen and IMAX theaters on the 18th.

Twists and Turns to Pique All Interests

“Thunderbolts,” “The Accountant 2,” and “Star Wars: Episode III Revenge of the Sith” are some upcoming blockbusters that will hopefully wow audiences. Looking for a date-night flick? There are high hopes for “Pride & Prejudice,” a 20th-anniversary re-release, which comes out on April 20. Adam Sandler fans, your time will come; ”Happy Gilmore 2” hits the Netflix library on July 25.

Before that, TC fans will look forward to “Mission: Impossible: The Final Reckoning” on May 23. Meanwhile, the major Independence Day blockbuster may be “Jurassic World Rebirth.” Sticking with this '90s jaunt, “I Know What You Did Last Summer” aims to hook in horror-film aficionados beginning on July 18.

The Bottom Line

So, there’s really something for everyone in the months ahead. Theater traffic caught a boost recently after a sluggish start to 2025, and the international plot is even more compelling. This holiday weekend may kick off a busy spring and summer box office season. Consumer spending trends, just like some big forthcoming releases, will be watched.

1 Domestic Yearly Box Office, Box Office Mojo, April 15, 2025, Domestic Yearly Box Office
2 Domestic Yearly Box Office, Box Office Mojo, April 15, 2025, Domestic Yearly Box Office
3 Disney wins the 2024 box office as year-end receipts offer a welcome boost, CNBC, Sara Whitten, January 15, 2025, Disney wins the 2024 box office as year-end receipts offer a welcome boost
4 Wall Street is watching for a $10 billion domestic box office in 2026. Disney could get it there, CNBC, Sarah Whitten, February 2, 2025, https://www.cnbc.com
5 Ne Zha 2, Box Office Mojo, April 15, 2025, Ne Zha 2
6 Demon-child movie wows China – and smashes global box office records, The Guardian, Amy Hawkins, March 16, 2025, Demon-child movie wows China - and smashes global box office records
7 Domestic Yearly Box Office, Box Office Mojo, April 15, 2025, Domestic Yearly Box Office

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Tesla: Sell Now Or Regret Later
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) is in a crisis mode as its sales are falling, the brand image is shattered, and the future looks bleak as a result of the ongoing trade war. The company’s shares are already down over 20% since we published our latest article on the company nearly two months ago, and we believe that the worst is not over yet and there’s still room to fall. We also don’t expect Tesla to report decent Q1 results on April 22, as its quarterly deliveries already took a major hit in recent months, while the increase in macroeconomic risks will likely limit its upside in the following months.

The Damage Is Done

The Q1 delivery numbers that were released earlier this month showed that Tesla is in a crisis mode. During the first quarter of the year, the company delivered only 336,681 vehicles, down from the estimates of 377,000 vehicles, which is one of the worst quarterly delivery numbers in recent years. What’s worse for Tesla is that its deliveries have fallen at a time when the EV market is booming, and its legacy peers are reporting a rise in EV sales in Q1. As a result of this, we don’t expect Tesla to release decent earnings results next week when plans to reveal its Q1 report.

While in part the decline could be attributed to the changeover of Model Y lines across some of its factories, it would be foolish to believe that Elon Musk’s involvement in politics had nothing to do with it. In recent months, there have been multiple protests against Elon Musk, which have negatively affected Tesla’s sales. In addition to that, J.P. Morgan stated that the damage to Tesla’s brand has been unprecedented and lowered the company’s price target to only $120 per share.

Although there’s a possibility that Elon Musk will step down from its role in the government, we believe that the damage to Tesla’s brand has been made, and it will be hard for the company to overcome the challenges that it faces. At the same time, the escalation of the global trade war will likely limit Tesla’s upside even more.

As of now, a significant portion of Chinese imports into the United States are tariffed at 145%, while the rest of imports are tariffed at up to 245%. At the same time, American imports into China are now tariffed at 125%. Such a sudden increase in trade barriers between the biggest economies in the world will more than likely undermine the growth of the global economy and will also affect Tesla’s sales and performance in the upcoming months. After all, the company’s production plans for Cybercab and Semi are already under a threat of disruption due to the rising cost of components.

In China, the future for Tesla doesn’t look bright as well. Before the escalation of the trade war by the Trump administration earlier this month, Tesla’s sales were already declining in China due to the rising competition and the ongoing price war there. Now the situation is likely to deteriorate further as Tesla stopped taking orders in China for its flagship models due to the escalation of the trade war.

In Europe, things are not going better. Tesla’s sales in major European markets have also mostly been declining largely because of Elon Musk’s involvement in politics not just in the United States but in some countries of the European Union as well. The company is now also required to pay a 7.8% tariff on its Chinese-made cars that are exported to the European Union, which will likely further undermine Tesla’s ability to perform well on the continent in the foreseeable future.

Largely because of all of those developments, Tesla has received numerous downward revisions in recent months. We believe that those revisions are justified and think that the upcoming earnings report for Q1 is going to disappoint investors considering the latest weak delivery numbers.

The Path To Recovery Is Still There

While Sino-American relations are unlikely to improve anytime soon, the potential pause of separate auto tariffs could be a good thing for Tesla. The company still sources a decent percentage of automotive parts from Mexico and Canada, while Elon Musk himself admitted that the tariffs will still have a significant impact on Tesla. That is why the potential pause could help the company avoid an outright disruption of its North American supply chain and somehow find a way to improve its sales back home.

Tesla’s bet on autonomy could also yield great results over time and help the company offset macro-related risks. In June, the company expects to release the unsupervised FSD offering in Austin, which should move Tesla closer to achieving its robotaxi goals over time. In addition to that, if the company manages to overcome the tariff-related challenges and reaches its goal of ramping up the production of autonomous Cybercabs next year, it will be able to improve its delivery numbers in the future.

Considering this, Tesla’s upcoming releases of autonomous products and features could nevertheless help the company mitigate some of the macro risks that it currently faces.

The Real Value of Tesla

The fall of deliveries along with the trade war made us change some of the assumptions in the valuation model ahead of the earnings release. The previous model showed that Tesla’s intrinsic value is $81.56 per share. Following the latest developments, we believe that the real value of Tesla is even lower.

The change of some of the assumptions is happening at a time when Tesla is trading at $254.11 per share, the forecast period still covers the next five years, the perpetual growth rate remains at 3%, and the cash and total debt numbers have been taken from the latest earnings report.

Tesla’s discount rate in our valuation model stands at 10.46%. We figured it out by calculating the cost of debt primarily by using the TTM numbers, and the cost of equity where the risk-free rate is 4.40%, the beta stands at 1.84, and the market return rate is 7.77%.

In the forecast table below, one of the biggest changes happened with the sales assumptions, which were decreased by 5% in comparison to the last model in all fiscal years. This is because the fall in deliveries shows that the sales momentum is waning, while the trade war will likely have a negative long-term effect on Tesla’s performance. For the current fiscal year, we expect only a 10% Y/Y growth rate in sales, which is similar to the latest expectations.

The EBIT margin is unchanged for now. This is because there’s a possibility that the upside from autonomy could offset the tariff-related downside in the upcoming months and years. At the same time, we wouldn’t rule out a major downward revision of margins after the Q1 results are released if the management decreases its own outlook, as it would likely signal that the impact from the trade war is going to be more meaningful than expected.

The bottom part of the forecast table mostly remained unchanged from the previous model.

This new model shows that Tesla’s intrinsic value is only $67.12 per share, below our previous calculations, and below the current price by around 73%. We wouldn’t consider it to be an unrealistic estimate of Tesla’s real value since under our updated assumptions the company is expected to make between $4 billion and $5 billion in FCF in 2025. This is already an increase from $3.6 billion in FCF last year. Also, Tesla currently trades at around 100 times its forward earnings while the sector’s median is around 13x, which shows that the company indeed is greatly overvalued and its stock price is significantly detached from reality.

Final Thoughts

Tesla faces so many challenges at once that we don’t see a path to a major recovery of its stock anytime soon. Because of the increased macroeconomic risks, its upside is now even more questionable, while the downside remains significant. That is why we don’t expect much from the upcoming earnings report that’s scheduled to be released on April 22 and continue to rate Tesla as a Sell.

data.title: Why Does Volatility Often Lead To Strong Emerging Equity Returns?
data.description:
Emerging-market (EM) stocks might not seem an obvious choice for anxious investors during a trade war. But history suggests that past volatility peaks have created favorable moments to invest in EM stocks.President Donald Trump’s tariff agenda has fueled extraordinary market volatility. Given that many EM countries have significant exports to the US, you might think that EM stocks would be relatively weak. Yet this year through mid-April, the MSCI Emerging Market Index fell by less than the S&P 500. In our view, this indicates that much of the bad news from tariffs was already priced into EM assets while US stocks needed to adjust.

Understanding the Fear Factor

But that’s just the very short term. What can we expect in the coming months? While past performance never guarantees future results, history suggests that EM stocks have performed relatively well after spikes in market turbulence.The VIX Index, an index of US equity market volatility, is widely known as the fear index. We looked at market returns after different month-end VIX levels since December 2000 (Display). Extreme VIX readings are uncommon; the index only exceeded 40 nine times at month-end during the 24-year period surveyed above.When the VIX ended the month between 40 and 50 - indicating heightened anxiety - EM stocks returned more than 64% in the subsequent 12-month period on average, well ahead of developed-market (DM) stocks. At even more volatile moments, when the VIX breached 50, EM stocks performed even better over the next 12 months, delivering a 69.2% return on average and widening the gap with their DM counterparts, which returned 34.7%.It sounds counterintuitive, especially since EM stocks are generally perceived to be riskier than their DM counterparts. So how can we explain this observation? In our view, it comes down to market psychology. Markets often overreact to negative news and bad news gets priced in. When the VIX touches extremely high levels, we think it indicates that investors fear the worst. Yet often, the future turns out better than worst-case scenarios.

Are Things Different This Time?

Today, the fear factor is being driven by real uncertainties as investors struggle to price in the macro and micro impacts of tariffs on economies and companies. Near-daily changes to Trump’s policy decisions make it particularly hard for companies and equity investors to forecast earnings. A recession is possible, and tariffs could go higher.On the other hand, deals could get struck, stimulus packages may be announced and a recession—which may well be underway—will eventually end.Nobody can say how the trade war will play out. If it expands, there could be effects that we haven’t seen in our lifetimes as professional investors. Yet we do know that it’s almost impossible to time market inflection points and staying invested increases the chances of long-term success. If EM equities do follow historical patterns, we think investors in strategies that target companies with strong fundamentals and the ability to withstand tariff pressures could be rewarded for their patience and perseverance when the dust eventually settles.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Inflation Risk Is Subsiding Rapidly
data.description:
Inflation risk has been a significant topic of discussion in the mainstream media for the last few years. Such is unsurprising given that inflation spiked following the pandemic in 2020 as consumer spending (demand) was shot into overdrive from stimulus payments and production (supply) was shuttered. To understand why that occurred, we need to revisit “Economics 101.”

“In economics, inflation is a general increase in the prices of goods and services. Changes in inflation are a function of fluctuations in actual demand for goods and services (also known as demand shocks, including changes in fiscal or monetary policy or recession), changes in available supplies such as during energy crises (also known as supply shocks), or changes in inflation expectations, which may be self-fulfilling. Note that supply and demand are key facets of the inflation equation.

Basic economics states prices will be set at a level where the supply of goods or services meets consumer demand.”

The economic illustration shows this basic principle taught in every “Econ 101” class. As noted, in 2020, inflation was the consequence of restricting supply and massively increasing demand.

That massive surge in stimulus sent directly to households resulted in an unprecedented spike in “savings,” creating artificial demand. As shown, the “pig in the python” effect is evident. Over the next two years, that “bulge” of excess liquidity has reverted to the previous growth trend. Given that economic growth lags behind the reversion in savings by about 12 months, we should continue to see economic growth slow into 2025. Notably, the “lag effect” is critical to the “inflation risk” thesis.

Understanding that inflation is solely a function of supply and demand, the ongoing reversal of monetary liquidity is continuing to erode economic activity. Notably, what caused the inflation spike post-2020 was not an increase in the debt or the Federal Reserve but rather the temporary increase in the money supply caused by sending checks to households. Therefore, the inflation risk will continue to subside unless the government passes a new infrastructure spending bill of massive proportions or sends another stimulus to households.

“But Lance, tariffs are inflationary.”

They aren’t for two reasons, and it all starts with consumer confidence.

Consumer Is The Key To Inflation Risk

I understand the basic assumption that if you impose a tax on a product, good, or service, then the “cost” of that product, good, or service will increase, hence the inflation risk. While that is perfectly logical, it excludes two crucial factors: 1) Only producers pay the “tax” from tariffs, and 2) we measure inflation (in terms of CPI) from the consumer side of the equation.

In “Tariffs Aren’t An Inflation Risk,” we discussed tariffs’ impact on the production side of the equation.

“Post-pandemic demand surges, supply chain disruptions, and massive fiscal and monetary interventions supported those elevated margins. As evidenced by the chart below, the correlation between economic growth rates and corporate profits is high. Note that outliers of the correlation are historically related to events such as the “Financial Crisis” and post-recession economic recoveries.”

Corporations react to cost increases in their business (i.e., wages, benefits, commodities, utilities, etc.), which must be factored into the selling price to maintain profitability. Crucially, corporations can only pass on higher input costs to consumers if demand remains higher than the available supply of those goods or services. In 2020 and 2021, corporations could pass on most of the inflationary increase to consumers as they were willing to spend the Government’s money. However, as excess savings run out, inflation declines as consumers decrease spending; corporate profits weaken as the ability to pass on higher input costs to customers fades. As shown, as inflation declines, the rate of change in corporate profits also weakens.”

Read that bolded sentence again.

When discussing inflation risk, consumer activity drives inflationary pulses in the economy. If we use a two-year average of corporate profits minus inflation, we can visualize that impact. As shown, inflationary increases, like tariffs, are only inflationary in the economy if they can be passed onto the consumer. Inflation surged in 2020 as corporations could pass on the bulk of the cost increases to consumers flush with cash. Today, to consumers. Today, inflation is declining due to declining demand. As such, the percentage of cost increases corporations must absorb is increasing, which reduces corporate profitability but shows up in the economy as slowing inflation.

Here is the crucial point:

“Corporations don’t create inflation. They merely react to changes in demand and adjust pricing and supply to maintain profitability. When the consumer slows down, corporations cut prices to reduce supply.”

As we should expect, consumer actions, which is how we measure inflation through the consumer price index (CPI), drive inflation risk. Consumer confidence is the key to understanding whether inflation risk is present in the economy.

Consumers Lack Confidence

Despite all the commentary about tariff-related inflation risk, inflation is hard to achieve if consumers are unwilling or, more importantly, unable to pay higher prices. As noted in this past week’s commentary, “Consumers Are Tapping Out,” consumers show signs of deep financial stress.

“At the heart of the problem is the collapse of household balance sheets in the lower-income and middle-income brackets. These groups have depleted the excess savings accumulated during the pandemic and are turning to high-interest borrowing to bridge the gap. The Philadelphia Federal Reserve reported that the share of active credit card accounts making only minimum payments surged to 10.75% in Q3 2024—a record high. This statistic isn’t just a warning about credit health; it points to widespread cash flow stress.“

Furthermore, consumer confidence in finding employment continues to erode as the economy slows. Given that employment creates income for consumption, it is difficult to expand consumption (demand) if consumers do not have a job, fear losing their jobs, or wage growth stagnates.

We can investigate this further by examining Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE), which comprise nearly 70% of the economic equation. Historically, when consumer confidence is declining, consumption also slows.

As such, it is unsurprising that inflation is tied to consumer confidence. As consumer confidence declines, the demand for goods and services also declines. The reduction in economic activity shows up in the current inflation risks.

Conclusion

Lastly, Consumer stress isn’t limited to anecdotal indicators—it shows up in corporate earnings and executive commentary. During the company’s earnings call, Doug McMillon, CEO of Walmart, stated that many customers are under “budget pressure.” They also exhibit “stressed behaviors,” including spending reductions across general merchandise. Specifically, he warned that “For many customers, the money runs out before the month does.”

Similarly, Dollar General CEO Todd Vasos painted an equally concerning picture. He described his customers as “struggling more than ever before.” Todd added that some are now forgoing non-discretionary items, like medication or hygiene products, to afford groceries and fuel. Hesaid, “These customers are making trade-offs we haven’t seen in years.” Concurring with that warning was Jane Fraser, CEO of Citigroup. She observed that consumers are “becoming more cautious” and focusing spending on smaller, lower-cost purchases. While this signals a growing defensive posture, often associated with recessionary conditions, they are also deflationary. When consumer behavior shifts en masse from aspirational to survival-based, the ripple effects are inevitable.

The bottom line is that inflation risks are extremely muted given the rapidly slowing economic backdrop and disruption in the stock and bond markets, which also impact consumer confidence. Could that change? Yes, but such a change would require a reinstatement of stimulus checks, a surge in Government spending, and the Federal Reserve increasing monetary policy. For now, none of those are available.

The most significant risk to the economy is not the return of inflation risks but rather the collapse in consumer confidence that leads to a recession.

We may have that data showing up sooner than later.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Metro: A Market Outperformer You're Probably Overlooking
data.description:
Metro Inc. (TSX:MRU:CA) (OTCPK:MTRAF) stock, while not the hottest stock, has been greatly outperforming the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) this year, with a 13.5% YTD total return in Canadian dollars and a 17.8% total return in USD compared to the SPY's negative 9.88% return.

This is likely because MRU:CA is seen as a safe-haven stock during tough times since Metro is a food and pharmacy retailer. It operates grocery stores like Metro, Super C, and Food Basics, as well as pharmacies like Jean Coutu.

So, is Metro stock a strategic Buy in this type of volatile environment? It could be, and its recent earnings results (which I'll discuss) were solid, but I'd rather wait for a dip before considering a purchase. Even just a 5-6% dip – as long as the macroeconomic environment remains somewhat volatile – could provide a solid buying opportunity to outperform the market.

Currently, the valuation is a tad elevated compared to historical norms, and the stock is on an upswing. That's why I rate MRU:CA as a Hold for now, although it's a recession-resistant company with more growth ahead despite all the recent economic drama.

A Brief Overview Of Metro's Q2 2025 Results

Metro's Q2-2025 results were solid. Adjusted diluted EPS of $1.02 grew 12.1% year-over-year and just missed the consensus estimate of $1.025 (per TradingView). Meanwhile, its revenue reached $4.91 billion, beating the $4.86 billion estimate and growing by 5.5% YoY.

Further, the company's gross margin ticked slightly higher from 19.9% to 20% for Q2. Similarly, operating expenses as a percentage of sales declined a tiny bit from 10.7% to 10.6% despite things like higher energy costs and increased online partnership fees.

Other notable highlights from the earnings report include Food same-store sales growth of 5.3%, or 3.9% when adjusted for the two-day Christmas shift, and Pharmacy same-store sales growth of 7%. In the Pharmacy segment, there was a 7.8% increase in prescription drugs and a 5.3% increase in front-store sales, but after adjusting for the two-day Christmas shift, the front-store sales increase was 3.7%.

For the quarter, free cash flow was $309.8 million, while TTM's free cash flow sits at $1.1565 billion.

Also, Metro continues to buy back shares, with 2.849 million shares being bought at an average price of $92.65 between November 27, 2024, and April 4 of this year for a total of $264 million. On top of that, management reiterated its medium- to long-term EPS growth target of 8% to 10%, so the company is clearly confident in its future.

What Can Drive Metro's Growth?

The Pharmacy segment is one of the avenues that can drive growth for Metro. Management mentioned that over 2.4 million clinical acts and services were performed in the first 24 weeks of this fiscal year. This momentum could continue or even accelerate when Bill 67 is implemented in Quebec, which is expected to happen within the next year. The bill would allow pharmacists to take on more clinical responsibilities, which could lead to more service revenues.

During the Q2 earnings call, Jean-Michel Coutu, President of The Pharmacy Division, said:

So Bill 67, we were expecting it sometime this spring. There's been some delays as they work through the regulations and finalizing the scope. So we're expecting the regulations to pass sometime in June. Now that said, the AQPP is still negotiating pharmacist compensation. So right now, the way we look at it is we think sometime in probably Q1 -- late Q4 or early Q1 that we'll start getting the benefits, the full benefits, of Bill 67. That's kind of our outlook on that portion of the business right now.

For the Grocery business, Metro can see strength in its discount grocery stores like Super C and Food Basics as people look to save money in hard times. Besides that, management noted that the company is seeing more interest in Canadian-made products, possibly due to the "support Canadian" mentality.

Its online grocery business is another area of growth, as it grew 26% year-over-year. Even though management stated that the e-commerce segment is dilutive to margins, they see it as a "loyalty play," as the customers that shop online are also shopping in-store.

Finally, Metro, like many other grocers, is investing in automation supply chain upgrades, which can help boost margins and efficiency.

Metro Stock's Valuation Is A Bit Elevated Relative To Its Past

Right now, Metro's TTM P/E ratio is around 22.8x, based on its TTM adjusted EPS of $4.49. The image below shows a P/E ratio of 23.6x, but I believe the chart hasn't been updated for the most recent earnings report. Nonetheless, I've included the graphic to show the stock's historical P/E ratio. It's easy to see that its valuation multiple keeps expanding.

The 10-year EV/EBITDA multiple chart below also shows an elevated valuation, with a 13.56x multiple compared to the 11.84x average.

In a risk-off environment, an elevated multiple can be sustained because people will flee to safer stocks. But once things start to look better again, the valuation can drop closer to more normalized levels. For a longer-term buy, I'd prefer to wait for a lower valuation, say, a 19-20x P/E ratio, which would leave much less room for a valuation contraction.

Nevertheless, in the current environment, a 5-6% drop in share price could be enough to warrant a buying opportunity, as long as you believe things will stay volatile. However, that'd be more of a tactical and technical-based investment rather than a valuation-based one, in my view. A 5-6% drop would bring the share price to the $96 range and would be in line with a short-term uptrend.

Plus, it's not as though the stock has gone straight up through these volatile times. There have been a few small dips to buy, so I wouldn't get FOMO and try to buy right now.

Have Tariffs Affected Metro So Far?

It seems that tariffs have had a minimal impact on Metro – at least so far. During the Q2 call, Metro's CEO said, "The recently introduced tariffs and counter-tariffs did not impact food inflation in the second quarter."

Additionally, vendors are helping the situation by shifting sourcing, like moving berry supply from the United States to Mexico.

The company is also seeking alternative suppliers and working with partners to delay price increases. CEO Eric La Fleche stated:

So yes, we’ve received some cost increases related to counter-tariffs. We asked for 6 weeks’ notice from those suppliers. So in mid-April, starting now, some small cost increases related to tariffs can start to be reflected. But we’ve been working with our vendors to justify, to provide government codes. So whatever is subject to counter-tariff, we try to measure as best we can to minimize the cost for us and the retail price for the customer. But we’re working with them, and they want to protect – some of them want to protect their volumes.

Overall, while tariffs haven't had enough time to take full effect yet, Metro looks well-positioned to handle them. And it's a defensive play regardless, so investors will still flock to it.

The Takeaway

Metro has been outperforming the market during these volatile times, and its Q2 results were relatively strong. It's a reliable company with lots of free cash flow, share buybacks, and a good amount of long-term growth potential.

That said, the valuation is a bit elevated, but Metro's recession-resistant business makes it a stock worth watching right now. A 5-6% pullback would be enough to get me interested in the stock for a tactical play amid market volatility, while a larger pullback would present a good entry point for long-term investors seeking stability.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Slate Grocery REIT: This High-Yielder's Fundamentals Keep On Improving
data.description:
Slate Grocery REIT (TSX:SGR.UN:CA)(OTC:SRRTF) continues to make progress on a fundamental level, but shares remain cheap because investors are still skeptical. It creates an opportunity to lock in a 9.3% yield that should become more and more sustainable as the company executes its plan.

(Slate is a TSX-listed REIT that reports results in USD. All references are in USD unless noted otherwise)

Introduction

Slate Grocery REIT is the owner of 116 grocery-anchored retail real estate developments, which collectively span 15.3M square feet of gross leasable space. The portfolio is spread across 23 states, with approximately 50% of assets located in just four states – Florida (16%), North Carolina (13%), New York (11%), and Georgia (7%).

57% of the portfolio is located in the Sunbelt region of the United States, an area that is growing faster than the rest of the country due to better weather, greater employment opportunities, and generally lower taxes.

Slate's top tenants include many of the most prominent names in U.S. grocery. Kroger is the REIT's top tenant, followed closely behind by Walmart. Other top tenants include Ahold Delhaize, Publix, and Albertsons.

We'll note that 68% of the portfolio comes from essential tenancies—including grocery, health care (including pharmacies), banks, and restaurants. Rents from anchor tenants account for nearly half of total revenues.

Grocery-anchored real estate is attractive for a few different reasons. Firstly, supermarkets don't move around that often. Usually, a grocer will protect a good location as much as they possibly can. This gives the landlord a certain amount of pricing power. Supermarkets also attract a lot of foot traffic, which benefits other tenants in the same development. In addition, more grocers are offering delivery or in-store pickup using existing stores as fulfilment and pick-up centers.

Grocery stores are also recession-resistant. When the rest of the economy is struggling, consumers are more likely to cook at home versus going to a restaurant.

When I last analyzed Slate Grocery REIT, I noticed that fundamentals were getting stronger. Both funds from operations (FFO) and adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) were improving, buoyed mostly by strong leasing spreads. This, combined with an improving balance sheet, an attractive dividend, and a dirt-cheap valuation, made the stock an interesting choice.

Approximately six months after my analysis, it's easy to argue that the company has actually gotten stronger, yet shares are down approximately 5%. Let's take another look at this one, focusing on the company's improvement so far, and its potential for even better results for the rest of 2025 and into 2026.

Operational update

Slate released its fourth-quarter earnings in February, and results were generally solid.

For the quarter, rental revenue was up 3% and net operating income (NOI) was up 3.3%. Same-property NOI was even stronger, increasing by 4.9% on a year-over-year basis.

Leasing spreads were also strong, with the total leasing spread (this includes both new leases and renewals) increasing by 14% on a year-over-year basis.

On the negative side, new leasing activity disappointed a little bit, with only a little over 93,000 square feet of new leases negotiated in the quarter. In the same period last year, Slate managed more than 160,000 square feet of new leases.

Both FFO and AFFO were down in the quarter, falling to $0.25 and $0.20 per unit, respectively. In the same quarter in 2023, the REIT earned $0.27 per share in FFO and $0.22 per share in AFFO. The decrease was largely caused by one-time financing charges and a higher-than-expected tax bill, two things that aren't expected to repeat going forward.

In the conference call with analysts, Slate's management revealed several factors that should help grow the bottom line in 2025.

The big factor is leasing spreads. Slate has a low in-place rent versus the market average. It is currently getting $12.65 per square foot, while the average in the marketplace is $23.80. That's a discount of nearly 50%, with the discount even larger when compared to new development rents.

Attractive leasing spreads were the main driver behind Slate's improved NOI in Q4. Management expects the trend to continue, telling an analyst that the company has already put up seven consecutive quarters of double-digit leasing spreads. That momentum, plus Slate's already low in-place rents, bodes well for the rest of the year.

Slate has a total of 1M square feet worth of leases renewing in 2025, and it'll follow that up with more than 1.8M square feet of leases expiring in both 2026 and again in 2027. Note the low average in-place rent compared to market values.

The company had a significant amount of debt to refinance in 2024, which was something investors were keeping an eye on. Things looked dicey for a little while, but Slate did manage to refinance approximately $630M in 2024. It ended up paying around the same interest rate, too.

It has about 13% of the total debt due for refinancing in 2025. Many investors are expecting rate cuts in 2025, with the market pricing in a 98% chance of at least one rate cut between now and the end of the year. The most likely scenario sees rates falling either 75 or 100 basis points.

This should help the company refinance its existing debt at a good rate. It also bodes well for the debt it has coming due in 2027—perhaps it could even be proactive and renew that debt early if rates are attractive in 2026.

Finally, I'll note the possibility of the company repurchasing shares. On January 29th Slate Grocery REIT got permission from the Toronto Stock Exchange to repurchase up to 10% of its Class U units. That gives it the option to repurchase more than 5.5M shares.

As of today, I wouldn't expect much in the way of share repurchases. Most of Slate's earnings go towards paying its generous dividend, and the rest are earmarked for capital expenditures. It could take on more debt to fund share repurchases, but the balance sheet is a little stretched. The current debt-to-assets ratio is above 52%.

There is the possibility that the company could ramp up some share repurchases in the second half of the year as FFO improves or if it makes some non-core dispositions, but investors shouldn't expect anything more than modest share repurchases.

Valuation

No matter how you view it, Slate Grocery REIT is cheap.

Let's start with the company's price-to-net asset value (NAV). Slate's NAV at the end of its last quarter was $13.84 per unit. Its USD-denominated shares on the Toronto Stock Exchange currently trade hands at $9.89 each. That's a discount of 29% versus NAV.

Another way to measure how cheap Slate is versus its peers comes from its implied value on a price-to-square-foot basis. As of February 11th, Slate was trading at a value of $111 per square foot of leasable space. At the same time, its peers traded at a range of between $213 per square foot and $530 per square foot. That's almost a 50% discount compared to its cheapest peer, and a 79% discount compared to the most expensive comparable.

Slate's view of this phenomenon is quite simple. The stock is a "compelling investment opportunity" according to CEO Blair Welch, especially when investors consider the upside it has from raising rents.

Finally, let's look at a more traditional way to value Slate—on a price-to-FFO basis. Analysts expect the REIT to earn $1.11 per share in FFO in 2025, with that number increasing to $1.16 per share in 2026. That gives us forward valuations of 8.9x and 8.5x for 2025 and 2026, respectively.

Slate's peers, meanwhile, mostly trade at forward price-to-FFO valuations in the 11-15x range. That's a significant discount versus comparable REITs.

Dividend sustainability

If Slate can execute its plan to raise rents without a hiccup, it translates into good news for the dividend.

In 2024, Slate earned $1.10 per share in FFO. It paid $0.072 per unit on a monthly basis in distributions (just over $0.86 annually), which works out to a 79% FFO payout ratio.

The payout was tighter on an AFFO basis, with that payout ratio averaging about 98% for the year.

If Slate can execute its plan and deliver double-digit leasing spreads on the 750,000+ square feet of non-anchor space it has coming up for renewal in 2025, this will drive FFO on a per-share basis higher. There's also potential for the REIT to improve the bottom line in 2026 as well, with a total of 1.8M square feet of both anchor and non-anchor leases coming up for renewal.

In short, if Slate continues to improve fundamentals—and signs are pointing towards that outcome—then the distribution will slowly become more and more affordable. That bodes well for the current 9.3% yield the stock offers investors. It's unlikely we'll see a distribution hike anytime soon, but the payout ratio creeping lower is a good thing. It frees up capital for the company to buyback shares or pay down debt, among other things.

Once investors see a little more wiggle room there, it's likely they'll start getting a little more excited about the stock.

Risks

There are two major risks I'll highlight.

The first is operational. Much of the bull thesis comes from the company being able to increase rents for both existing and new tenants. But there are things that can go wrong with that strategy. Tariffs could impact the retail space in a big way. A weaker-than-expected economy could force some of Slate's secondary tenants to shut down locations, too.

The second risk comes from interest rates. Rates are unpredictable. Investors expect the Federal Reserve to cut rates in 2025, but there's no guarantee that could happen. Higher than expected interest rates would likely put pressure on the share price, as well as increase the cost of upcoming debt renewals.

The bottom line

Slate Grocery REIT has been slowly improving its operations for a while now, and we're starting to see improved results.

If the company can continue to execute its plan to increase in-place rents, both FFO and AFFO should steadily march higher. This isn't just a 2025 thesis, either. Leases for 24% of total leasable space are due to be renewed in 2026 and 2027, too. It's a multi-year thesis.

Plus, investors could get a break as the marketplaces offer a greater value on boring assets—like Slate's portfolio of grocery-anchored real estate.

I continue to be bullish on the stock. I believe the company's plan to increase earnings is a sound one, which will eventually translate into increased distribution security, an improved valuation, and, most importantly, a higher share price. I maintain my buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Introducing EGLE And FLAG: An Innovative Approach To U.S. Equity Investing
data.description:
On April 16, 2025, we listed the Global X S&P 500 U.S. Revenue Leaders ETF (EGLE) and the Global X S&P 500 U.S. Market Leaders Top 50 ETF (FLAG). Both EGLE and FLAG take an innovative approach to U.S. equity investing by seeking to provide exposure to companies that are not only domiciled in the U.S. but also derive the majority of their revenue domestically.

While the S&P 500 has arguably been the most popular way to access broad U.S. equity exposure for decades, the rise of globalization contributed to an increase in international revenue streams for large U.S. companies. With escalating, tit-for-tat trade wars and a broad shift towards global fragmentation, investors may benefit from a narrower approach to U.S. equity investing to provide a potential level of insulation from the disruption stemming from these trends. A more tailored approach to domestic equities may help avoid these pitfalls while simultaneously identifying opportunities in a growing U.S. economy.

Key Takeaways

  • Globalization has contributed to an increase in foreign revenue streams for large U.S. companies, potentially necessitating the reconsideration of what constitutes exposure to the U.S. economy.
  • The U.S. has been the fastest growing major advanced economy since COVID, amplifying the arguments for U.S. exposure .1
  • Pairing a U.S. revenue focus alongside quality screens could potentially reduce portfolio risk while targeting exposure towards companies poised to benefit from U.S. economic growth.

Traditional U.S. Equity Exposure May Be Due for a Revamp

The digitization of the economy, growing consumer spending in emerging markets, and a prolonged period of relative global stability have broadly encouraged U.S. companies to invest in international distribution. This shift was reasonable for many companies, as Asia accounts for more than half of the world’s consumers, representing a key growth market for U.S.-based companies.2 The trend in the globalization of revenue has advanced to the point where more than a quarter of the constituents in the S&P 500, the most widely tracked index for large-cap U.S. equities, generate the majority of their revenue outside the United States.

Despite the potential benefits, the globalization of revenue for large U.S. companies can carry risks that investors may not consider. While country and currency risks are generally evaluated based on the domicile of each constituent for an index, revenue exposure may offer a more complete view. For example, investments domiciled in Emerging Markets are often subject to heightened risks from political instability, reduced liquidity, and elevated volatility. U.S. domiciled companies with substantial Emerging Market revenue may carry many of the same risks, as they face the possibility of unpredictable regulatory burdens as well as hurdles with disposing of assets or repatriating earnings.

The risk of currency fluctuations are often considered short-term in nature, as economic theory suggests that currency valuations mean revert over time.3 While this is true in theory, in practice currency trends can persist for longer than the investment horizon for many individuals, which is ultimately the time frame that matters most. These risks can affect U.S.-domiciled companies, as revenue outside the United States generally leads to the receipt of foreign currency. If U.S. dollars appreciate relative to those foreign currency receipts, companies may have to recognize a translation loss on their income statement, reducing their earnings. If the trend in U.S. dollar appreciation persists over time, investors may be forced to reevaluate the worth of the company.

This is salient for investors today, as the primary measure of dollar strength, the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY), has seen substantial appreciation over the past 20 years.4 Over that timeframe, three distinct time periods can be observed where there is some degree of reversion back to a local mean, but there has not been a trend back towards some time-period agnostic value. While the past may not offer any direct insight into future currency movements, the last 20 years demonstrate that trends can persist beyond an investor’s time horizon. This suggests that considering revenue exposure, in addition to domicile, may benefit investors seeking U.S.-centric exposure.

Past performance is not a guarantee of future performance.

As we move into a period that may be less defined by globalization and instead by global fragmentation, companies with global customer bases may be at more risk in an escalating tariff environment. In 2024, the U.S. federal government collected approximately $83b from customs duties.5 Since his inauguration, President Trump has promised to substantially increase that figure and has either proposed or implemented substantial tariff increases across top U.S. trade partners and products. The value of these additional tariffs is expected to reach over $200b in 2025 and increase to over $320b over the next decade.6 This marks a shift away from the promotion of free global trade that has proliferated since the founding of the World Trade Organization in 1995 and represents a potential risk for companies with global revenue exposures.

EGLE: A Tailored Approach to U.S. Equities

The Global X S&P 500 U.S. Revenue Leaders ETF (EGLE) targets constituents of the S&P 500 that derive the majority of their revenue from the United States. EGLE’s goal is to track, before fees and expenses, the performance of the S&P 500 U.S. Revenue Leaders Index. The initial index universe is composed of S&P 500 constituents, which represent 500 of the largest and most influential U.S. companies, collectively accounting for roughly 80% of the total U.S. market capitalization.

From that initial universe, S&P applies a screen to exclude any company that generates the majority of its revenue from outside the United States. By tracking the resulting index, EGLE offers investors a vehicle to take a selective approach to U.S. equity investing. This approach accounts for not just the trends in globalization that have taken place in recent decades but also offers the potential to reduce some specific risks that can stem from global revenue exposure.

Identifying Quality Opportunities in the U.S.

Companies that have prioritized domestic growth may have benefitted as the U.S. demonstrated stronger economic growth in the last five years than other major developed economies.7 Relative to Canada, which is ranked second for economic growth in the G7 over the last five years, the U.S. grew its economy more than 1.8 times faster. In Europe, the gap is even more dramatic, ranging from 2.7x more than Italy to 10x faster growth than Germany since 2019. Companies that generate the majority of their revenue in the U.S. may be better positioned to benefit relative to peers with a greater focus on global growth should this trend continue.

While several factors have contributed to the economic growth differential between the U.S. and other major advanced economies, a few catalysts are at the forefront. First are technology investments, as U.S. domiciled hyperscalers have poured over $750b into capital expenditures since 2020.8 This includes massive data centers and the chips to power them, as well as research and development investments to improve their respective suites of large language models. Outside of the private sphere, federal investments into areas like chip manufacturing and infrastructure through the passage of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, the CHIPS Act, and the Inflation Reduction Act have helped support U.S. competitiveness on the global stage. Finally, consumer spending, which represents 68% of gross domestic product in the U.S., has remained robust since the recovery from the COVID-19 pandemic.9 Since January 2021, the month that real personal expenditures recovered to pre-pandemic levels, consumer spending has grown over 15% in the U.S. on an inflation-adjusted basis.10 Looking ahead, we see the potential for these favorable dynamics to continue.

Introducing FLAG: Quality Delivered from the U.S.A.

The Global X S&P 500 U.S. Market Leaders Top 50 ETF (FLAG) invests in companies that are constituents of the S&P 500 (excluding those in the real estate sector), derive at least 50% of their revenue from the United States, and rank among the top 50 eligible companies by Market Leader Score—a metric based on sustained free cash flow (FCF) margin, sustained return on invested capital (ROIC), and market share. FLAG seeks to track, before fees and expenses, the S&P 500 U.S. Revenue Market Leaders 50 Index.

Sustained ROIC and FCF are empirically supported quality metrics that help identify companies that consistently generate shareholder value while maintaining resilient earnings. Quality as a factor is often employed within a portfolio in an effort to reduce the impact of bear markets, as companies with strong internal cash flow generation and strong balance sheets can often better withstand poor economic conditions than peers. The addition of market share into the Market Leader Score and the heightened U.S. focus may also position FLAG differently than traditional quality investments. Companies with a higher Market Leader Score should generally generate strong internal cash flows and have a high market share in their respective businesses. This may assist in identifying companies that are positioned to capitalize on a potential continuation of outsized growth in the U.S.

Glossary

1. Free Cash Flow Margin: The ratio of free cash flow generated over a period divided by sales. Free cash flows are calculated as operating cash flow less capital expenditures.2. Return on Invested Capital: The ratio of net operating profit after taxes divided by invested capital.3. G7: A grouping of the seven major developed economies: Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the United Kingdom, and the United States.4. Hyperscalers: Large cloud service providers with substantial computing power and infrastructure to support major applications and services.
